aliases.name: Man-Monkey of the Shropshire Union Canal

description.summary: A black, ape-like figure with large white eyes reported on the Shropshire Union Canal at Bridge 39, near Norbury Junction on the Staffordshire–Shropshire border. The original 1879 encounter was recorded by folklorist Charlotte Sophia Burne in 1883.
description.full: The Man-Monkey is a humanoid cryptid or apparition associated with the Shropshire Union Canal in the English Midlands, particularly with Bridge 39 at the Staffordshire–Shropshire border. The original account, recorded by Charlotte Sophia Burne in Shropshire Folk-Lore (1883) on testimony collected in 1879, describes a labourer driving a horse-cart from Ranton (Staffordshire) to Woodcock (near Newport, Shropshire) on the night of 21 January 1879. As he reached the canal bridge, 'a strange black creature with great white eyes' sprang out of the roadside plantation and onto his horse's back. The man eventually reached home in great fright; a local police-officer he later met reportedly told him that he was lucky not to have met the 'Man-Monkey' that had been seen there before. The story was revived in modern cryptozoology and Fortean writing by Jonathan Downes and Nick Redfern, who collected later 20th-century reports along the same stretch of canal.

etymology.origin: English; coined in the 19th-century Shropshire/Staffordshire countryside.
etymology.meaning: 'Man-Monkey' — a humanoid figure with ape-like features.
etymology.first_use: 1879 (oral, in Shropshire/Staffordshire); first published 1883 in Burne and Jackson's Shropshire Folk-Lore.

related_phenomena.name: Bigfoot
related_phenomena.description: North American hairy hominid cryptid often paralleled with British 'wildman' reports

features.description: Reports describe a 'strange black creature' or 'huge black, hairy monkey'

physical.morphology: A short, dark, hairy humanoid figure with prominent eyes, roughly the size and shape of a person but with monkey-like features.

abilities.description: Said to spring suddenly from the roadside or canal-side onto passers-by or their animals
Vanishes after the encounter, typically without trace

patterns: Encounters cluster on or near Bridge 39 of the Shropshire Union Canal

range.description: Reports are localized to the Shropshire Union Canal corridor near the Staffordshire–Shropshire border, especially the area around Norbury Junction and the canal bridge identified as Bridge 39.

hotspots.name: Bridge 39, Shropshire Union Canal

location.description: Bridge 39 (locally known as Big Bridge), Shropshire Union Canal, near Norbury Junction

hotspots.notes: Site of the original 1879 encounter and of multiple later 20th-century reports.

first_recorded.source: Charlotte S. Burne and Georgina F. Jackson, Shropshire Folk-Lore (1883)

peak_activity_periods.description: Modern boater and walker reports along the same stretch of canal, revisited by Jonathan Downes and Nick Redfern.

origins.cultural_roots: English Midlands folklore of canals, road-bridges, and 'boggart'-like apparitions; rural ghost-lore.
origins.earliest_accounts: Burne's 1883 Shropshire Folk-Lore, recording an 1879 incident.
origins.folklore_connections: Charlotte Burne herself connected the Man-Monkey to medieval and classical werewolf and bogey traditions; the story sits between ghost-lore and 19th-century proto-cryptozoology.

timeline.event: A labourer driving a cart from Ranton to Woodcock is attacked at a Shropshire Union Canal bridge by a black creature with great white eyes that springs onto his horse's back
Charlotte Sophia Burne and Georgina F. Jackson publish Shropshire Folk-Lore, including the Man-Monkey account
Nick Redfern publishes Man-Monkey: In Search of the British Bigfoot, collecting later reports and reviving the case
timeline.source: Redfern, Man-Monkey (CFZ Press, 2007 ed. ISBN 978-1905723164; original work earlier)

history.modern_era: The Man-Monkey is a fixture of British cryptozoology and Fortean writing, particularly through the work of Jonathan Downes (Centre for Fortean Zoology) and Nick Redfern. The Canal & River Trust's 2002 appeal for canal ghost stories produced further accounts of a black, ape-like figure on the same stretch of canal.

notable.name: Bridge 39 horse-cart encounter (1879)

location.description: Shropshire Union Canal bridge on the road from Ranton to Woodcock (later identified as Bridge 39, near Norbury Junction)

credibility_factors: Recorded close to the event by a recognised folklorist

notable.description: A labourer was returning at about ten o'clock at night with a horse-cart from Woodcock back toward Ranton when, just before the canal bridge, 'a strange black creature with great white eyes' sprang from a roadside plantation onto his horse's back. The labourer struck at it with his whip, which passed through the figure 'as through smoke', and reached home in great alarm. A police-officer he encountered the following day reportedly told him not to be frightened — it was only the 'Man-Monkey' which had been seen there before.
evidence: Eyewitness testimony recorded by Charlotte S. Burne

documentary.name: Burne and Jackson, Shropshire Folk-Lore (1883)
documentary.description: The published 1883 record of the 1879 Bridge 39 encounter, the foundational text of the Man-Monkey case.
documentary.analysis: Charlotte Burne herself read the story in light of medieval werewolf and bogey traditions; modern cryptozoologists have re-read it as a proto-cryptid report.

paranormal.name: Ghost / apparition
paranormal.description: Burne records that the labourer's whip passed through the figure 'as through smoke', and proposes that the Man-Monkey may be the ghost of a person drowned in that stretch of canal, or a survival of werewolf-like folklore.
evidence_cited: Insubstantial response to physical contact in the 1879 account

scientific.name: British cryptid hominid
scientific.description: The Man-Monkey is treated by some modern cryptozoologists as part of a British 'wildman' or 'British Bigfoot' reporting tradition.
evidence_cited: Pattern of black, ape-like reports along the same stretch of canal across more than a century

skeptical.name: Folkloric / boggart tradition
skeptical.description: The Man-Monkey is best understood as a 19th-century English folk apparition — a 'boggart' of the canal corridor — rather than as a flesh-and-blood animal.
proponents: Folklorists working in the Burne tradition
evidence_cited: Folkloric framing by the original recorder

traditions: Shropshire and Staffordshire ghost / boggart tradition
stories: The 1879 Bridge 39 encounter as recorded by Charlotte S. Burne
folklore.beliefs: Locally, Bridge 39 retains a reputation as a haunted spot among boaters and walkers on the Shropshire Union Canal.

literature: Charlotte S. Burne and Georgina F. Jackson, Shropshire Folk-Lore (1883)
Nick Redfern, Man-Monkey: In Search of the British Bigfoot (CFZ Press, 2007)
other: Frequently featured on canal-folklore tours and in British Fortean magazines

researchers.name: Charlotte Sophia Burne
researchers.contribution: Recorded the foundational 1879 Bridge 39 encounter in Shropshire Folk-Lore (1883)

researchers.name: Nick Redfern
researchers.contribution: Modern survey of Man-Monkey reports along the Shropshire Union Canal

researchers.name: Jonathan Downes
researchers.contribution: Editorial and field work on British cryptid reports including the Man-Monkey

organizations.name: Centre for Fortean Zoology
organizations.focus: British cryptozoology and Fortean field research

research.ongoing_studies: Ongoing collection of reports along the Shropshire Union Canal corridor by independent Fortean and cryptozoological researchers.

sources.title: Shropshire Folk-Lore: A Sheaf of Gleanings
Man-Monkey: In Search of the British Bigfoot
The Man-Monkey of Shropshire Union Canal
